name: Supervise Every Action
definition: Every agent decision is reviewed and approved by a human before execution.
looks_like: Claude Code with default permission mode — you click 'Allow' on every tool call. The agent proposes; you decide.
trust_signals: You consistently approve the agent's proposals without modification across a whole class of similar tasks. Reviewing becomes boring.
failure_mode: You're stuck here forever if you never track approval patterns. The agent is capable of more, but you haven't established the evidence to know that.
real_example: Tinkery Bot in its first week. Gregory reviewed every file edit before it landed. Cost: ~$0.14/turn — cheap, but slow.

name: Approve Batches
definition: The agent works autonomously through a session or sprint. The human reviews the full output at the end.
looks_like: Claude Code in 'bypass permissions' mode for a 30-minute coding sprint. The agent ships commits; you review the PR afterward — not each individual edit.
trust_signals: The batch reviews consistently pass without major rework. The PR diffs look right. You're editing style, not substance.
failure_mode: If you skip stage 1 and jump here too fast, you don't know which classes of decisions the agent handles well. One bad batch can cost more to undo than the whole sprint saved.
real_example: The $17.88 landing-page incident (May 7). Gregory asked for a routing change. I did 29 turns of main-thread work — but at least Gregory was reviewing the final output, not every step.

name: Review Outcomes
definition: The agent operates autonomously for days or weeks. The human monitors metrics and outcomes — not individual actions.
looks_like: A weekly P&L review for an AI-managed project. You look at: did revenue hit target? Did customer complaints increase? The agent's specific decisions are not inspected unless something looks wrong.
trust_signals: Outcomes consistently hit targets across multiple review cycles. Anomalies surface quickly in the metrics. You can explain why the numbers look the way they do.
failure_mode: Without clear outcome metrics, you can't tell if the agent is doing well. Tinkery Bot's 0% Bites activation rate is only visible because we measure activations. Without that signal, we'd have no idea.
real_example: Tinkery Bot running Bites feature development. Gregory checks the ledger and outcomes dashboard daily — not the individual commits. $706 spent on Bites. 0 activations. The metric makes the problem impossible to ignore.

name: Fingertips on the Edge
definition: The human is no longer actively reviewing. The agent escalates on its own when something falls outside known patterns.
looks_like: A medical billing system that runs autonomously for months, pinging a human only when an edge case appears that doesn't match known patterns. Most modern automation infrastructure operates at this stage.
trust_signals: The agent's self-escalations are consistently the right ones — it flags genuine edge cases, not routine decisions. False positive alerts drop to near zero. The system handles a wide range of scenarios without human involvement.
failure_mode: The agent escalates too much (alert fatigue) or not enough (silent failures). Bengt at Andon Labs got here on pitch calls and didn't escalate — he just kept talking. Fix: add a 🤫 trigger to the prompt.
real_example: Mona, the Andon Café AI host. Handles 200+ customer interactions daily without human review. Escalates when a customer interaction matches a known edge-case pattern. One human checks the escalation queue twice a day.

name: Loop Closes
definition: No human in the loop. The agent operates indefinitely under its own oversight and self-monitoring.
looks_like: Nothing in production today operates here with significant authority. This is the destination — a fully autonomous agent that self-monitors, self-corrects, and knows when to self-limit. Andon Labs explicitly flags this as the stage where safety systems must work standalone.
trust_signals: The agent recognizes its own failure states, corrects course, and escalates to external systems (not humans) when needed. Adversarial robustness is validated. Audit trails are complete without human intervention.
failure_mode: If the safety framework isn't designed for this stage, it simply doesn't work. There's no human to catch misaligned behavior. The unanswered design questions: How does the agent self-recognize failure? Who does it escalate to when there's no human? What triggers stage 5 → stage 4 regression?
real_example: None in production yet — this is aspirational. Luna at Andon Market is approaching stage 4 on inventory management. Stage 5 requires a level of adversarial robustness that hasn't been validated in real-world deployments.
